title: Sole bonne femme
subtitle: sole fillets poached in white wine with mushrooms and a velouté sauce
excerpt: Dover sole fillets poached in white wine with shallots and mushrooms, then finished with a cream-enriched velouté made from the poaching liquid. Sole bonne femme is an elegant French classic that rewards careful timing: the fish needs barely six minutes in the oven and the sauce comes together in under ten.
sourceNotes:
A. Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903). Sole bonne femme, the full method including the fish velouté reduction and the cream finish. The 'bonne femme' garnish of mushrooms and shallots is documented here as a classic preparation. Public domain.

Auguste Colombié, Nouvelle Cuisine Bourgeoise (1906). Home version of sole bonne femme with simplified velouté method. Public domain.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The mushroom and shallot base can be prepared up to 2 hours ahead and kept covered. Make the velouté and poach the fish just before serving.

glossaryTerms.definition: One of the French mother sauces, made from a light roux (butter and flour) finished with a white stock (fish, chicken, or veal) rather than milk. In sole bonne femme, the velouté is made from the poaching liquid left by the fish, which concentrates the flavour of wine, shallots, and mushrooms.

A French culinary term meaning 'good woman' or 'good wife', used to describe a simple, homely style of cooking. In fish dishes, the bonne femme garnish refers to shallots and mushrooms cooked in butter and white wine alongside the fish.

content.text: Sole bonne femme is Dover sole fillets poached in white wine with the
garnish of shallots and mushrooms. The poaching liquid is then reduced and enriched with cream to make a

that is poured back over the fish. Nothing in the dish is technically demanding, but the result tastes expensive and cared-for. It is a good recipe to know when you want to cook something that reads as special without spending a great deal of time in the kitchen.

Dover sole has a firmer, sweeter flesh than lemon sole and holds together better under gentle heat. If Dover sole is unavailable, lemon sole or plaice fillets will work, but reduce the poaching time by a couple of minutes as they are more delicate. The mushrooms should be sliced thinly so they cook through in the short time they spend in the oven alongside the fish.

items.prepNote: Dover sole preferred; 2 fillets per person, about 80 to 100 g each

content.text: A wide, shallow ovenproof sauté pan that can go from the hob into the oven is the most useful piece of kit here. If you do not have one, use an ovenproof baking dish for the poaching and transfer the liquid to a saucepan for the sauce.

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Melt half the {{unsalted-butter}} of cooking butter in a wide ovenproof sauté pan over medium heat. Add the {{shallot}} shallots and cook for 3 to 4 minutes until soft and translucent. Add the {{chestnut-mushrooms}} of mushrooms and cook for 3 minutes, stirring, until they soften and release their liquid. Pour in the {{dry-white-wine}} of white wine and bring to a simmer for 2 minutes.

Season the {{sole-fillet}} sole fillets with salt and white pepper and lay them in the pan, folded in half if they are large, in a single layer over the mushroom and shallot base. Add enough water to just cover the fish, about 100 to 150 ml. Dot the remaining cooking butter over the fish. Cover with baking paper pressed directly onto the surface, then transfer to the oven for 6 to 8 minutes until the fish is just white and barely cooked through. Lift the fillets carefully onto warm plates using a fish slice and cover loosely to keep warm.

Strain the poaching liquid through a sieve into a small saucepan, pressing the mushrooms and shallots to extract the juice. Reserve the mushrooms. Bring the liquid to a boil and reduce by about half, which should take 3 to 4 minutes. In a separate small pan, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} of sauce butter and stir in the {{plain-flour}} of plain flour; cook for 1 minute to make a pale roux. Add the reduced poaching liquid gradually, whisking constantly to make a smooth sauce. Stir in the {{double-cream}} of double cream and let the sauce simmer for 2 minutes until it coats the back of a spoon. Add the reserved mushrooms and shallots back in. Season with salt, white pepper, and a squeeze of {{lemon}} lemon juice.
Spoon the sauce over the sole fillets on the warm plates. Scatter with {{parsley-flat}} of flat-leaf parsley. Serve at once with steamed new potatoes or plain boiled rice to soak up the sauce.

items.symptom: Sole fillets break apart when lifted from the pan
items.cause: Overcooked, or not lifted with proper support
items.fix: Sole is delicate; 6 minutes is usually enough. Slide a wide fish slice flat beneath each fillet and lift in one smooth movement; if the fillet is folded, unfold it gently on the plate

items.cause: Poaching liquid not reduced enough, or too much water added to the pan
items.fix: Reduce the liquid harder before making the velouté. It should be well-flavoured and concentrated before the roux goes in

items.cause: Liquid added too quickly to the roux
items.fix: Remove from heat and whisk vigorously; if lumps persist, pass through a fine sieve. Next time add the liquid in small additions and whisk between each one

items.cause: Not enough seasoning, or wine was too neutral
items.fix: Season the fish before poaching, not just the sauce; a squeeze of lemon over the finished plate also lifts the flavour considerably. Use a wine with some character

content.text: Sole bonne femme is part of the classical French repertoire codified by Escoffier, though the combination of sole, white wine, shallots, and mushrooms is older. The 'bonne femme' label in French cooking means something plain and domestic: the food a home cook makes, not a chef showing off. Sole meunière is better known outside France, but bonne femme is more rewarding to make. The dish builds its own sauce from the poaching liquid, giving a layered flavour that pan-frying alone cannot achieve.
